title: Negotiating a Used Bicycle Price
summary: A buyer meets a seller to inspect a second-hand bicycle, and a polite test ride turns into a real negotiation about hidden faults and a fair final price.

body.speaker: Buyer
body.text: Hi, you must be Marcus. I'm Lily — I messaged about the bike. Thanks for keeping it for me.

body.speaker: Seller
body.text: Hello, Lily. No problem at all. It's just out the back, leaning against the fence. Come and have a proper look before you decide.

body.speaker: Buyer
body.text: I appreciate that. Before I test ride, can you tell me how long you've owned it and why you're selling?

body.speaker: Seller
body.text: Three years. I bought it new in 2022, used it for commuting until last winter, and then I moved closer to work and stopped needing it. It's been in the garage since February.

body.speaker: Buyer
body.text: That's useful to know. Has it had any major repairs?

body.speaker: Seller
body.text: One. The rear wheel was replaced last summer after a bad pothole. The rest is the original setup. I'd say it's been serviced about once a year, more or less when it needed it.

body.speaker: Buyer
body.text: Do you have any of the receipts?

body.speaker: Seller
body.text: Most of them, yes. I keep that kind of thing. I can email them after we're done if you'd like.

body.speaker: Buyer
body.text: That would help. Can I take it round the block?

body.speaker: Seller
body.text: Of course. Please do. Take fifteen minutes if you need to. I'd rather you found problems now than feel cheated later.

body.speaker: Buyer
body.text: Right, I'm back. I noticed a few things. The front brake is sticking slightly, the chain skipped twice on the second gear, and the saddle has a small tear I didn't see in the photos.

body.speaker: Seller
body.text: Honest feedback, thank you. The brake I can probably fix in five minutes — I just haven't gotten around to it. The chain skipping, I'd want to look at properly. The saddle, I'll be upfront, I forgot to mention.

body.speaker: Buyer
body.text: I think the bike is good, but those three things add up. You're asking three hundred pounds. Given the condition, would you accept two hundred and forty?

body.speaker: Seller
body.text: That's quite a drop. Let me think aloud. The wheel was new last summer, the frame is in excellent shape, and the original price was over six hundred. Two-forty feels low. Where did you get to that number?

body.speaker: Buyer
body.text: Honest answer? A new saddle is about thirty pounds. A chain inspection, probably twenty. The brake might be free if it's quick, but if it's the cable, that's another twenty. So I'm building in about seventy pounds of work plus a small discount for uncertainty.

body.speaker: Seller
body.text: That's actually fair. I hadn't thought of it that way. I'd hoped to get two hundred and seventy. Could we meet at two hundred and sixty, given that I'll send you the receipts and the original tools?

body.speaker: Buyer
body.text: Two-sixty works for me, on one condition. If the chain turns out to need replacing rather than adjusting, would you be willing to take twenty pounds off after the inspection? I'll send you the bill.

body.speaker: Seller
body.text: I'd rather agree on a final number today, honestly. How about two-fifty cash, and we both forget about the chain whatever it turns out to be?

body.speaker: Buyer
body.text: Done. Two-fifty cash, and you send the receipts. Are you happy with that?

body.speaker: Seller
body.text: I'm happy. To be honest, you negotiated more thoughtfully than most people who come round. Most just say 'two hundred' and hope. You did your homework.

body.speaker: Buyer
body.text: I learned the hard way. Last bike I bought, I didn't test ride and ended up paying a hundred pounds to fix things I should have spotted.

body.speaker: Seller
body.text: That's how everyone learns. Right — I'll grab the original lock and the manual, you give me the cash, and I'll send the photos and receipts tonight.

body.speaker: Buyer
body.text: Perfect. One last thing — would you mind sending the receipt for the wheel replacement separately? In case I have any future warranty issue.

body.speaker: Seller
body.text: Good thinking. I'll send that one first. Thanks for being so straight about it all. Enjoy the bike.

annotations.exampleEn: Always take a test ride before buying a second-hand bike.

annotations.meaningVi: đi lại đến nơi làm việc
annotations.exampleEn: I used it for commuting until I moved closer to work.
The rest is the original setup from when I bought it new.
I've serviced the bike about once a year.
Can I take it round the block to see how it rides?
I'd rather you found problems now than feel cheated later.

annotations.meaningVi: xích bị nhảy (chuyển số không mượt)
annotations.exampleEn: The chain skipped twice on the second gear.

annotations.meaningVi: yên xe có một vết rách nhỏ
annotations.exampleEn: The saddle has a small tear that wasn't in the listing photos.
I haven't gotten around to fixing the brake yet.
I'll be upfront — I forgot to mention the scratch.

annotations.meaningVi: ba điều đó cộng dồn lại
annotations.exampleEn: Each fault is small, but those three things add up.
Let me think aloud for a moment before I answer.

annotations.meaningVi: tính gộp vào, đưa vào tính toán
annotations.exampleEn: I'm building in about seventy pounds of work into the price.

annotations.meaningVi: gặp nhau ở (mức giá thoả thuận)
annotations.exampleEn: Could we meet at two hundred and sixty?

annotations.meaningVi: đã chuẩn bị kỹ, đã tìm hiểu
annotations.exampleEn: You clearly did your homework — that was a fair offer.
Keep the receipt in case of any future warranty issue.

grammarNotes.title: Second Conditional for Polite Negotiation
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Hội thoại sử dụng câu điều kiện loại 2 và 'would' để thương lượng một cách lịch sự: 'Would you accept two hundred and forty?', 'If the chain turns out to need replacing, would you be willing to take twenty pounds off?', 'I'd rather agree on a final number today'. Cấu trúc: 'would + verb' (hoặc 'if + past simple, would + verb'). Khi đề nghị hoặc đặt câu hỏi nhạy cảm, dạng giả định mềm mỏng hơn câu hỏi trực tiếp.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: The dialogue uses second conditional and 'would' to negotiate politely: 'Would you accept two hundred and forty?', 'If the chain turns out to need replacing, would you be willing to take twenty pounds off?'. Form: 'would + verb' (or 'if + past simple, would + verb'). For sensitive proposals, the hypothetical form softens the request.

grammarNotes.title: Present Perfect for Recent History of an Object
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Người bán dùng thì hiện tại hoàn thành để mô tả lịch sử gần đây của chiếc xe: 'It's been in the garage since February', 'Has it had any major repairs?', 'The rear wheel was replaced last summer', 'I've serviced about once a year'. Cấu trúc: have/has + V3. Dùng khi mô tả các hành động đã xảy ra với một đối tượng vẫn còn tồn tại và liên quan đến hiện tại.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: The seller uses the present perfect to describe the bike's recent history: 'It's been in the garage since February', 'Has it had any major repairs?', 'I've serviced about once a year'. Form: have/has + past participle. Use when describing actions on an object that still exists and matters now.

translationVi:
Người mua: Chào, chắc anh là Marcus. Tôi là Lily — tôi đã nhắn về chiếc xe. Cảm ơn anh đã giữ lại cho tôi.

Người bán: Chào Lily. Không có gì. Xe ở phía sau, dựa vào hàng rào. Vào xem cho kỹ trước khi quyết định.

Người mua: Tôi cảm ơn. Trước khi đi thử, anh có thể nói anh đã sở hữu xe bao lâu và vì sao bán không?

Người bán: Ba năm. Tôi mua mới năm 2022, dùng đi làm đến mùa đông năm ngoái, rồi tôi chuyển đến gần chỗ làm và không cần nữa. Xe ở trong gara từ tháng Hai.

Người mua: Hữu ích để biết. Xe có sửa chữa lớn nào chưa?

Người bán: Một lần. Bánh sau được thay mùa hè năm ngoái sau một cú ổ gà nặng. Phần còn lại là cấu hình nguyên bản. Tôi bảo dưỡng khoảng một lần mỗi năm, hoặc khi cần.

Người mua: Anh còn giữ các hoá đơn không?

Người bán: Phần lớn, có. Tôi giữ những thứ đó. Tôi có thể gửi email sau khi xong nếu cô muốn.

Người mua: Sẽ giúp ích. Tôi đi thử quanh dãy phố được không?

Người bán: Tất nhiên. Cứ đi. Mười lăm phút cũng được. Tôi thà cô phát hiện vấn đề bây giờ còn hơn cảm thấy bị lừa sau này.

Người mua: Được, tôi về rồi. Tôi nhận ra vài chuyện. Phanh trước hơi kẹt, xích nhảy hai lần ở số hai, và yên xe có vết rách nhỏ mà tôi không thấy trong ảnh.

Người bán: Phản hồi thẳng thắn, cảm ơn. Phanh tôi có thể sửa trong năm phút — tôi chưa kịp làm. Xích nhảy, tôi muốn xem kỹ. Yên xe, tôi nói thẳng, tôi quên đề cập.

Người mua: Tôi nghĩ xe tốt, nhưng ba điều đó cộng dồn lại. Anh đòi ba trăm bảng. Với tình trạng này, anh có chấp nhận hai trăm bốn mươi không?

Người bán: Giảm khá nhiều. Để tôi nghĩ thành lời. Bánh mới mùa hè ngoái, khung tuyệt vời, giá ban đầu hơn sáu trăm. Hai-bốn-mươi nghe thấp. Cô tính ra con số đó thế nào?

Người mua: Câu trả lời thật? Yên mới khoảng ba mươi bảng. Kiểm tra xích, khoảng hai mươi. Phanh có thể miễn phí nếu nhanh, nhưng nếu cần thay dây thì hai mươi nữa. Vậy tôi tính khoảng bảy mươi bảng công sửa cộng một khoản giảm nhỏ cho sự bất định.

Người bán: Thật ra hợp lý. Tôi chưa nghĩ theo cách đó. Tôi hy vọng nhận được hai trăm bảy mươi. Mình gặp ở hai trăm sáu mươi nhé, với điều kiện tôi gửi cô hoá đơn và bộ dụng cụ ban đầu?

Người mua: Hai-sáu-mươi được, một điều kiện. Nếu xích cần thay chứ không chỉ chỉnh, anh có sẵn sàng giảm hai mươi sau khi kiểm tra không? Tôi sẽ gửi anh hoá đơn.

Người bán: Tôi thà thoả thuận con số cuối hôm nay, thẳng thắn. Hai-năm-mươi tiền mặt nhé, và cả hai cùng quên chuyện xích dù nó thế nào?

Người mua: Xong. Hai-năm-mươi tiền mặt, và anh gửi hoá đơn. Anh hài lòng chứ?

Người bán: Hài lòng. Thật ra, cô thương lượng cẩn trọng hơn hầu hết người đến. Hầu hết chỉ nói 'hai trăm' và hy vọng. Cô đã chuẩn bị kỹ.

Người mua: Tôi học qua bài học đau. Chiếc xe trước tôi không đi thử và cuối cùng trả một trăm bảng sửa những thứ lẽ ra phải thấy.

Người bán: Ai cũng học theo cách đó. Được — tôi sẽ lấy khoá nguyên bản và sách hướng dẫn, cô đưa tiền mặt, và tôi sẽ gửi ảnh và hoá đơn tối nay.

Người mua: Hoàn hảo. Một chuyện cuối — anh có phiền gửi hoá đơn thay bánh riêng không? Phòng khi có vấn đề bảo hành sau này.

Người bán: Suy nghĩ hay. Tôi sẽ gửi cái đó đầu tiên. Cảm ơn cô đã thẳng thắn về mọi chuyện. Chúc cô đạp xe vui vẻ.

questions.prompt: How long has Marcus owned the bike?
questions.explanation: Marcus says 'Three years. I bought it new in 2022.'

questions.prompt: Why is Marcus selling the bike?
options: He moved closer to work and stopped needing it
It is too damaged to use
questions.explanation: Marcus says 'I moved closer to work and stopped needing it.'
questions.hint: The reason is about commuting change.

questions.prompt: What major repair has the bike had?
options: The rear wheel was replaced last summer after a bad pothole
questions.explanation: Marcus says 'The rear wheel was replaced last summer after a bad pothole.'

questions.prompt: What three problems does Lily find on the test ride?
options: Flat tyre, broken bell, missing pedal
Front brake sticking, chain skipping on second gear, small tear in the saddle
Loose handlebars, broken light, cracked frame
Wobbling wheels, missing seat post, broken gears
questions.explanation: Lily says 'The front brake is sticking slightly, the chain skipped twice on the second gear, and the saddle has a small tear.'
Lily says 'You're asking three hundred pounds.'
questions.hint: The asking price is stated explicitly.

questions.prompt: How does Lily justify her offer of two hundred and forty?
options: By saying that's all she can afford
By breaking down repair costs — saddle, chain inspection, possible brake cable — plus a small discount for uncertainty
By saying other sellers offer lower prices
By saying it's the standard market rate
questions.explanation: Lily lists 'A new saddle... A chain inspection... if it's the cable, that's another twenty.'
questions.hint: Lily breaks down costs item by item.

questions.prompt: What price had Marcus hoped to get?
questions.explanation: Marcus says 'I'd hoped to get two hundred and seventy.'

questions.prompt: What price do they finally agree on?
questions.explanation: Marcus proposes 'two-fifty cash' and Lily says 'Done.'
questions.hint: The final agreed number is repeated by both speakers.

questions.prompt: What condition does Marcus attach to the final price?
options: He keeps the bike for another week
They both forget about the chain whatever it turns out to be
Lily comes back if she has problems
questions.explanation: Marcus says 'we both forget about the chain whatever it turns out to be.'
questions.hint: It is about closing the deal without future adjustments.

questions.prompt: What does Marcus say about Lily's negotiation style?
options: She negotiated more thoughtfully than most people, because she did her homework
questions.explanation: Marcus says 'you negotiated more thoughtfully than most people who come round... You did your homework.'
questions.hint: Marcus's comment is positive and specific.

questions.prompt: How did Lily learn to negotiate this way?
options: Her last bike — she didn't test ride and paid a hundred pounds to fix unspotted issues
questions.explanation: Lily says 'Last bike I bought, I didn't test ride and ended up paying a hundred pounds to fix things I should have spotted.'
questions.hint: Her learning came from a personal mistake.

questions.prompt: Why does Lily ask for the wheel receipt to be sent separately?
options: In case of any future warranty issue
questions.explanation: Lily says 'In case I have any future warranty issue.'
questions.hint: Lily plans for a possible future event.

questions.prompt: What overall negotiation approach does the dialogue model?
options: Pretend to walk away to pressure the seller
Honest, specific, evidence-based proposals from both sides leading to a fair compromise
Always start at half the asking price
Refuse to give any reasoning for your offers
questions.explanation: Both parties give specific reasons (repair costs, original purchase price, condition) and converge through transparent counter-offers.
questions.hint: Combine Lily's itemised breakdown with Marcus's reasoning about wheel and frame.

cloze.template:
Buyer: Hi, you must be Marcus. Thanks for keeping the bike for me. Before I test ride, can you tell me how long you've owned it and why you're selling?
Seller: Three years. I bought it new in 2022, used it for {{b1}} until last winter, and then I moved closer to work.
Buyer: Has it had any major repairs?
Seller: One. The rear wheel was replaced last summer after a bad {{b2}}. The rest is the original setup.
Buyer: Can I take it {{b3}} the block?
Seller: Of course. I'd rather you found problems now than feel cheated later.
Buyer: I'm back. The front brake is sticking, the chain {{b4}} twice on second gear, and the saddle has a small tear.
Seller: Honest feedback. I'll be {{b5}} — I forgot to mention the saddle.
Buyer: Those three things {{b6}} up. You're asking three hundred. Would you accept two hundred and forty?
Seller: Where did you get to that number?
Buyer: A new saddle is about thirty pounds. A chain inspection, twenty. I'm {{b7}} in about seventy pounds of work plus a small discount for uncertainty.
Seller: That's fair. I'd hoped to get two hundred and seventy. Could we {{b8}} at two hundred and sixty?
Buyer: On one condition. If the chain turns out to need replacing, would you take twenty pounds off after the {{b9}}?
Seller: I'd rather agree on a final number today. Two-fifty cash, and we both forget about the chain?
Buyer: Done. You negotiated more thoughtfully than most. You did your {{b10}}.

blanks.explanation: 'Commuting' is the exact verb — travelling to and from work specifically.
'Pothole' is the exact word — a hole in the road surface that damages bike wheels.
'Round the block' is the fixed phrase for a short circular test ride.
'Chain skipped' is the standard technical phrase — when the chain jumps off a cog under load.
'I'll be upfront' is the exact fixed expression — meaning honest in advance.
'Add up' is the standard phrasal verb meaning to accumulate into a larger total.
'Building in' is the exact phrasal verb — incorporating into a calculation.
'Meet at' (a price) is the fixed negotiation phrase — to compromise on a midpoint figure.
'Inspection' is the exact word the buyer uses — a formal professional check.
'Did your homework' is the fixed idiom for preparing thoroughly before a meeting.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Lily and Marcus reach a fair deal by being unusually open with each other. Why is honest negotiation rare even when both sides would benefit from it — and what makes a small transaction the right place to practise this skill?
